label: Yirmeyahu (Jeremiah) — Part 5 — Chapters 32–39
summary_plain: While Yerushalayim is under Babylonian siege, YHWH tells Yirmeyahu to buy a field in Anatot — a sign that houses and fields and vineyards shall again be bought in this land. YHWH promises to gather the people from all the countries where he has driven them and bring them back. Yirmeyahu speaks against Tsidqiyahu the king. The officials throw Yirmeyahu into the cistern of Malkiyahu — he sinks in the mud. Ebed-Melek the Cushite, a court official, goes to the king and says: These men have done evil by putting the prophet in the cistern. Ebed-Melek takes thirty men, pulls Yirmeyahu out with ropes and rags. YHWH promises Ebed-Melek: I will deliver you on that day — you shall not be given into the hand of the men you fear — because you have put your trust in me.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads from the DSS Jeremiah manuscripts (4QJer-a through 4QJer-e) and the Orit Ge'ez. Ebed-Melek the Cushite (chapters 38-39) is among the most significant figures in the prophetic literature — an African court official who rescues the prophet of YHWH and receives a personal divine promise of protection. His identity is textually confirmed: 'Ebed-Melek the Cushite' — Cushite meaning from the land of Cush (Nubia/Ethiopia). He operates at the highest level of the Judean royal court during the final siege. YHWH's specific promise to him (39:15-18) makes Ebed-Melek one of the few individuals in the prophetic corpus to receive a named, personal covenant guarantee. The field purchase at Anatot (chapter 32) is YHWH's sign of future restoration — performed during the destruction itself.

key_terms.definition: A Cushite (Ethiopian/Nubian) court official serving in the royal court of Yehudah. He rescued Yirmeyahu from the cistern where the officials had thrown him to die (38:7-13). YHWH personally promised him deliverance during the coming destruction (39:15-18). His name means 'servant of the king.'

From the land of Cush — the region south of Mitsrayim encompassing Nubia, modern Sudan, and Ethiopia. Ebed-Melek is identified as a Cushite — textually confirmed African identity at the apex of Judean political life.

During the Babylonian siege, YHWH commands Yirmeyahu to buy a field at Anatot (32:6-15). The purchase is a prophetic sign: houses, fields, and vineyards shall again be bought in this land. Restoration is promised in the midst of destruction.

Last king of Yehudah. He reigns during the Babylonian siege and is captured when Yerushalayim falls. His sons are killed before his eyes, then his eyes are put out. He is taken in chains to Babylon. Anglicized as Zedekiah.

fill_blank.prompt: When ______ the Cushite, a court official in the king's house, heard that they had put Yirmeyahu into the cistern.
fill_blank.source_quote: When Ebed-Melek the Cushite, a court official in the king's house, heard that they had put Yirmeyahu into the cistern.

fill_blank.prompt: Houses and fields and ______ shall again be bought in this land.
fill_blank.source_quote: For thus says YHWH of hosts, the Creator of Yisra'EL: Houses and fields and vineyards shall again be bought in this land.

fill_blank.prompt: I will surely deliver you, and you shall not fall by the sword — because you have put your ______ in me, declares YHWH.
fill_blank.source_quote: I will surely deliver you, and you shall not fall by the sword, but you shall have your life as a prize of war, because you have put your trust in me, declares YHWH.

multiple_choice.question: Who rescued Yirmeyahu from the cistern?
multiple_choice.source_quote: Ebed-Melek the Cushite, a court official, took thirty men with him and pulled Yirmeyahu up with ropes from the cistern.

multiple_choice.question: What did YHWH promise Ebed-Melek?
options: Personal deliverance because he trusted in YHWH
multiple_choice.source_quote: I will deliver you on that day — you shall not be given into the hand of the men you fear — because you have put your trust in me.

multiple_choice.question: Why did YHWH command Yirmeyahu to buy a field during the siege?
options: As a sign that restoration would come — fields would again be bought in the land
multiple_choice.source_quote: Houses and fields and vineyards shall again be bought in this land.

faq.question: What does Ebed-Melek the Cushite prove about African presence in the covenant community?
faq.answer: Ebed-Melek is documented in Yirmeyahu 38-39, confirmed in Qumran fragments. He is identified as 'the Cushite' — from the land of Cush, the region encompassing Nubia and Ethiopia. He is not a slave or a marginal figure. He is a court official (saris) serving in the royal palace of Yehudah during the final siege of Yerushalayim — the most critical moment in the kingdom's history. He has enough political authority to approach King Tsidqiyahu directly and to command thirty men for the rescue operation. He is named, he is high-status, he is African, and he is integrated at the apex of Judean political life in the 6th century BCE. YHWH personally guarantees his survival during the coming destruction (39:15-18). No European figure in the entire prophetic corpus receives a comparable named personal promise. The Great Eraser systematically minimizes Ebed-Melek — footnoting, de-emphasizing, or ignoring the African man who saved the prophet of YHWH.

faq.question: How does the cistern rescue parallel the covenant pattern?
faq.answer: Yirmeyahu is thrown into a cistern — a pit of mud and water — by the officials of his own people (38:6). He sinks into the mud and is left to die. An African man — Ebed-Melek the Cushite — defies the officials, goes to the king, obtains permission, and physically pulls the prophet out with ropes and rags placed under his arms to prevent injury (38:11-13). The pattern: the covenant prophet is betrayed by his own institutional leadership. The rescue comes from Africa. Yosef was thrown into a pit by his brothers. Moshe was drawn from the water by the daughter of Pharaoh. Yirmeyahu was pulled from the cistern by an African court official. The covenant narrative repeatedly documents that when the institutional community fails, the preservation comes through African agency. This is in the primary text.

faq.question: Why does Yirmeyahu buy a field during the destruction?
faq.answer: Yerushalayim is under siege. The Babylonian army surrounds the city. Yirmeyahu is in prison. And YHWH commands him to buy a field at Anatot — sign the deed, seal it, have it witnessed (32:6-15). The purchase is absurd by any material calculation: the land is about to fall to Babylon. But the prophetic sign is exactly this: 'houses and fields and vineyards shall again be bought in this land.' The covenant promise of restoration is made during the destruction — not after it. YHWH does not wait until the situation improves to promise restoration. The promise is spoken into the worst moment. For the covenant people in every generation of exile — scattered, displaced, apparently destroyed — the field purchase is the sign: YHWH promises restoration while the destruction is still happening. The dead deed in the sealed jar is the evidence of what YHWH will do.
